Abstract

Mitochondria generate ATP by creating a proton gradient across the inner membrane. This gradient also drives calcium uptake by the mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU) complex, while NCLX, encoded by SLC8B1, facilitates efflux. Mcu deficiency in various species does not result in obvious phenotypes, suggesting the existence of additional calcium transport systems. We here generated zebrafish lacking Tmbim5, a potential bidirectional mitochondrial calcium transporter, and Slc8b1, the probable NCLX ortholog. tmbim5 knockout fish exhibited impaired growth, muscle atrophy, increased brain cell death, and reduced mitochondrial membrane potential, but no significant changes in steady-state mitochondrial calcium levels. Slc8b1-deficient fish had increased larval mortality but were otherwise normal. Double knockouts of tmbim5 / mcu and tmbim5 / slc8b1 were viable with normal Mendelian distribution, indicating robust compensatory mechanisms maintain calcium homeostasis. tmbim5 knockout rescued the increased mortality in Slc8b1-deficient fish, and the NCLX inhibitor CGP-37157 lost its effect on behavior in Tmbim5-deficient but not Slc8b1-deficient zebrafish, implying shared, possibly antagonistic pathways. These findings suggest alternative pathways for mitochondrial calcium transport compensate for the loss of these proteins, enabling zebrafish survival.

Key words (5, alphabetical): In vivo Ca 2+ measurements / Ion fluxes / Mcu / Nclx / Tmbim5 /

Introduction

Mitochondria generate ATP by harnessing a proton and electron gradient generated by the electron transport system. The negative mitochondrial membrane potential across the inner mitochondrial membrane also acts as the driving force for the uptake of the universal cellular messenger calcium (Ca 2+ ). Increased mitochondrial Ca 2+ levels enhance oxidative phosphorylation, which leads to increased ATP production. Mitochondria also serve as cellular Ca 2+ sinks, making mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake important for cellular Ca 2+ signaling in general. Therefore mitochondrial Ca 2+ homeostasis is a finely tuned process that involves the coordinated action of Ca 2+ transporters, regulatory proteins, and interactions with the endoplasmic reticulum (ER).

The mechanisms governing mitochondrial Ca 2+ transport are not fully understood. The mitochondrial Ca 2+ channel mediating inward Ca 2+ flux is the mitochondrial Ca 2+ uniporter (MCU) (Baughman et al , 2011; De Stefani et al , 2011) which is part of a large protein complex which contains several MCU-interacting and regulatory proteins like MICU1 (mitochondrial uptake 1) (Mallilankaraman et al , 2012b), MICU2 (Patron et al , 2014), EMRE (essential MCU regulator) (Vais et al , 2016) and MCUR1 (mitochondrial Ca 2+ uniporter regulator 1) (Vais et al , 2015; Mallilankaraman et al , 2012a). Mcu knockout (KO) in mice leads to mild phenotypes in a CD1 background (Pan et al , 2013) but embryonic lethality in C57Bl/6 (Murphy et al , 2014). In zebrafish, mcu knockdown did not alter survival or activity in zebrafish larvae (Soman et al , 2017). Also mcu KO had no effect on viability, fertility, morphology or swimming behavior in zebrafish although it abolished mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake similar to the well-characterized MCU inhibiting compound ruthenium red (RuR) (Soman et al , 2019a; Tomar et al , 2019; Langenbacher et al , 2020). However, in vivo studies in zebrafish photoreceptors suggest alternative Ca 2+ entry pathways that can compensate for Mcu loss (Bisbach et al , 2020). One disputed candidate is Leucine Zipper And EF-Hand Containing Transmembrane Protein 1 (LETM1), identified by a genome-wide screen as a RuR-sensitive mitochondrial Ca 2+ /H + -antiporter (Jiang et al , 2009). Originally, LETM1 was described as part of the mitochondrial K + /H + exchanger (Nowikovsky et al , 2004). Ca 2+ efflux is primarily mediated by the Na + /Ca 2+ exchanger NCLX (Palty et al , 2010), whose knockout causes embryonic lethality in mice (Luongo et al , 2017). Recent evidence suggests that also Transmembrane Protein 65 (TMEM65) is involved in Na + -dependent Ca 2+ efflux from mitochondria (Vetralla et al , 2023; Zhang et al , 2023) and may act as a positive regulator of NCLX (Garbincius et al , 2023). Another mitochondrial Ca 2+ extrusion system is the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP), a non-selective high-conductance pore of debated identity (Bernardi et al , 2023; Altschuld et al , 1992). High mitochondrial Ca 2+ concentrations lead to opening of the mPTP which, when sustained, causes mitochondrial swelling, breakdown of mitochondrial membrane potential, and eventually cell death (reviewed in (Orrenius et al , 2015)).

Recently, a novel candidate for mitochondrial Ca 2+ transport emerged, the transmembrane BAX inhibitor motif containing protein 5 (TMBIM5) initially linked to the regulation of mitochondrial morphology, as loss of TMBIM5 in cells resulted in mitochondrial fragmentation and a disrupted cristae structure (Oka et al , 2008; Seitaj et al , 2020; Zhang et al , 2022; Austin et al , 2022). Loss of TMBIM5 was also observed to increase the sensitivity of cells to apoptosis (Oka et al , 2008; Seitaj et al , 2020; Patron et al , 2022). In vivo , mice genetically engineered with a specific alteration (D326R) that presumably disrupts the channel pore of the TMBIM5 protein (Bultynck et al , 2014) and leads to degradation of the protein (Zhang et al , 2022) exhibit several significant physiological and structural changes. These mice show a higher mortality during embryonic development or shortly after birth and develop a skeletal myopathy characterized by reduced muscle strength. These observations were closely linked to several mitochondrial abnormalities most dramatically obvious in mitochondria obtained from skeletal muscle. This included a disrupted cristae structure, premature mPTP opening, decreased ability of mitochondria to take up Ca 2+ , and swollen mitochondria (Zhang et al , 2022).

Two studies demonstrated that TMBIM5, when reconstituted in liposomes, can act as a Ca 2+ transporter in the presence of a pH gradient (Patron et al , 2022; Austin et al , 2022). Inhibiting NCLX-mediated mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux by CGP-37157 (CGP) (Austin et al , 2022) or NCLX knockdown (Patron et al , 2022) resulted in a diminished mitochondrial Ca² ⁺ retention capacity in TMBIM5 KO in human embryonic kidney (HEK) cells and reduced RuR-induced mitochondrial Ca² ⁺ efflux (Austin et al , 2022). TMBIM5 KO cells also accumulated more matrix Ca² ⁺ upon ER Ca 2+ release mediated by inhibition of the sarco/endoplasmic ATPase (SERCA) with thapsigargin (Patron et al , 2022) or stimulation of inositol trisphosphate receptor-mediated Ca 2+ release with ATP or carbachol (Austin et al , 2022). Concurrent knockout of MCU rescued elevated apoptosis induced by staurosporine in TMBIM5 -/-cells (Patron et al , 2022), suggesting that TMBIM5 can mediate Ca² ⁺ extrusion from mitochondria and that its function at least partially overlaps with NLCX. Zhang et al., in contrast, found that TMBIM5 overexpression in HEK cells enhanced mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake following ER Ca 2+ release (Zhang et al , 2022), suggesting that TMBIM5 may operate bidirectionally under certain conditions. This study also demonstrated that under steady-state conditions, loss of TMBIM5 results in increased potassium and reduced proton levels in the mitochondrial matrix suggesting a broader role in mitochondrial ion exchange (Zhang et al , 2022) in line with a potential interaction of TMBIM5 with LETM1 (Austin et al , 2022). The physiological significance of these mechanisms in vivo , especially the crosstalk of TMBIM5 with MCU and NCLX, is unclear.

Due to their translucency, zebrafish are an ideal tool to study mitochondrial Ca 2+ dynamics in vivo (Kettunen, 2020; Mandal et al , 2018). In the present study, we generated tmbim5 and slc8b1 KO fish by CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene editing (Gasanov et al , 2021) and observed that loss of Tmbim5 results in a muscle phenotype associated with delayed hatching, smaller size, and increased cell death in the brain while loss of Slc8b1 resulted in increased larvae mortality. In living larvae, Tmbim5 depletion did not affect mitochondrial Ca 2+ levels. CGP reduced swimming velocity and distance in wildtype but not in tmbim5 KO fish. To investigate potential interactions with known Ca 2+ transport systems, we generated tmbim5 / mcu and tmbim5 / slc8b1 double knockouts. Both lines were viable without major phenotypes. Concurrent Tmbim5 deletion actually reduced the mortality seen in slc8b1 KO larvae and did not exacerbate it. The mild phenotypes of double knockouts indicate either strong compensatory mechanisms or that additional Ca 2+ transport pathways maintain mitochondrial Ca 2+ homeostasis in the absence of these proteins.

Results

Tmbim5-deficient larvae are smaller and exhibit a reduced hatching efficiency

The zebrafish genome contains only one TMBIM5 orthologue which is expressed as early as six hours post fertilization (hpf) and plateaus after 24 hours (Fig. 1A). To study the effect of Tmbim5 deficiency, we generated mutant fish by introducing a deletion in exon 4 using CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing (Gasanov et al , 2021). This resulted in a profound decrease in tmbim5 mRNA (Fig. 1B); mutants ( tmbim5 -/-) reached adulthood and the Mendelian distribution was unchanged (Fig. 1C). Mutant larvae took longer to hatch than wild-type (WT, tmbim5 +/+ ) controls (Fig. 1D) and this correlated with reduced tail coiling (Fig. 1E), spontaneous movements of the tail that enable the embryo to escape from its chorion (Lessman & Carver, 2014). Locomotor activity of adult fish was unchanged (Fig. EV1C). The larger size of adult fish allowed us to perform a histopathological analysis of brain and muscle, the tissues with the highest mRNA expression levels of tmbim5 (Fig. 2B). A histopathological examination of brain tissue revealed no obvious differences (Fig. EV2A-D). However, a morphometric analysis of muscle fibers demonstrated a shift towards smaller fibers in the mutants (Fig. 2C) and a decline in the fibers with a cross-sectional area of 2500-3000 µm 2 (Fig. 2D). The average cross-sectional area of myofibrils was also reduced in tmbim5 -/-fish (Fig. 2D). Staining muscle tissue with Alcian blue revealed an increased staining of the intermuscular space (Fig. 2E-F). Alcian blue stains acid mucopolysaccharides, which can accumulate abnormally in certain muscle disorders. This staining is also regularly used to identify rimmed vacuoles or myxoid changes which can occur in certain myopathies and degenerative conditions (Tuckett & Morriss-Kay, 1988). Such enhanced Alcian blue staining was previously observed after knockout of the mitochondrial core transcription factor Tfam ( mitochondrial transcription factor A ) in vascular smooth muscle cells where this correlated with reduced oxygen consumption rates and increased lactate production (Oller et al , 2021). Masson's staining (Fig. EV2E-F) was unchanged, excluding the possibility that Alcian blue stained collagen fibers. This staining could reveal fibrosis that may occur as a result of myofiber degeneration (Van De Vlekkert et al , 2020). Furthermore, Alcian blue-Periodic Acid-Schiff (AB-PAS) staining revealed decreased glycogen levels in the muscles of adult tmbim5 -/-fish (Fig. 2F-G). This was also obvious in the liver of mutant fish (Fig. 2H). Together these results imply that Tmbim5-deficient fish have signs of metabolic impairment and muscle atrophy in line with the reduced muscle strength observed in larvae. The reduced weight of adult fish aligns with the reduced size observed in larvae.

Live imaging reveals unchanged mitochondrial Ca 2+ levels in the brain of Tmbim5-deficient fish

To clarify whether changes in the mitochondrial Ca 2+ homeostasis could explain the effects of Tmbim5 deficiency, we took advantage of the translucency of zebrafish larvae which allows the quantification of (mitochondrial) Ca 2+ levels in the living animal using genetically-encoded Ca 2+ sensors. We chose the hindbrain because tmbim5 is strongly expressed there as shown by whole-mount in situ hybridization (Fig. 3A); importantly no staining was observed in tmbim5 -/-larvae proving the specificity of the riboprobe. We also found increased cell death in this tissue as demonstrated by staining the brain of tmbim5 -/-larvae with Acridine Orange (Fig. 3B) implying functional changes associated with Tmbim5 deficiency. We crossed tmbim5 mutant with fish Tg ( CEPIA2mt ) expressing the mitochondrially targeted sensor CEPIA2mt (Suzuki et al , 2014) under the neuronal promoter HuC / elavl3 ( ELAV like neuron-specific RNA binding protein 3 ) and imaged mitochondria in the optic tectum, a dorsally located part of the hindbrain which is accessible for imaging in tmbim5 -/-; Tg ( CEPIA2mt ) and tmbim5 +/+ ; Tg ( CEPIA2mt ) larvae (Fig. 3A', Fig. 3C). To quantify total matrix Ca 2+ content, we exposed immobilized living zebrafish larvae to the uncoupling agent CCCP. This induces mitochondrial depolarization and, as a consequence, a release of Ca 2+ from mitochondria. No differences between tmbim5 +/+ and tmbim5 -/-larvae were observed implying that Tmbim5 deficiency in larvae had no major effect on mitochondrial Ca 2+ levels in vivo (Fig. 3C-D). Also inhibition of sodium-dependent Ca 2+ extrusion using CGP before CCCP treatment did not unmask any significant changes between WT and mutant larvae (Fig. 3C-D). Experiments boosting Ca 2+ influx with glutamate added to the bath were not successful. These results imply that in vivo matrix Ca 2+ levels under conditions of physiological cytosolic Ca 2+ levels are not affected by Tmbim5 even in the presence of CGP. To further investigate mitochondrial function in the brain of tmbim5 -/-larvae, we injected tetramethylrhodamine ethyl ester (TMRE) into the yolk of zebrafish embryos at the 1-cell stage as described in (Vicente et al , 2019) to determine whether loss of Tmbim5 affects the mitochondrial membrane potential. TMRE is a positively charged dye known to accumulate in mitochondria due to their membrane potential. Two days after injection, we observed a TMRE signal similar to the CEPIA2mt pattern (Fig. 3C), which was significantly reduced after depolarisation with CCCP (Fig. 3E). We observed a smaller drop in TMRE signal intensity in tmbim5 -/-larvae in response to depolarisation, indicating reduced dye accumulation within mitochondria and suggesting a reduced mitochondrial membrane potential in the brain of Tmbim5-deficient larvae (Fig. 3E). Together these findings suggest a potential mitochondrial dysfunction but no major differences in mitochondrial Ca 2+ concentration in Tmbim5-deficient fish.

The MCU complex is the major mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake mechanism (Baughman et al , 2011; De Stefani et al , 2011). To clarify whether Tmbim5, as overexpression results suggested (Zhang et al , 2023), can modify MCU activity or act as an additional Ca 2+ uptake mechanism besides the MCU complex, we crossed tmbim5 -/-mutants with mcu KO fish hypothesizing that depletion of two (potentially independent) uptake mechanisms would be detrimental and/or alter mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake. mcu KO fish lack the capacity to uptake mitochondrial Ca 2+ but have an otherwise unremarkable phenotype (Soman et al , 2019a). No differences in mortality were noted between tmbim5 +/+ ; mcu -/-and tmbim5 -/-; mcu -/-larvae (Fig. 4A) or in the distribution of adult zebrafish genotypes following a heterozygous cross (Fig. 4B). Mcu-depleted mitochondria had almost no Ca 2+ uptake with no significant further reduction in the absence of Tmbim5 (Fig. 4C) . Together these results imply that Tmbim5 does not constitute a mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake system independent of Mcu. p = 0.0422

No changes in the mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake machinery in tmbim5-deficient larvae

We next investigated the effect of tmbim5 deficiency on the expression of the mitochondrial Ca² ⁺ machinery and quantified the mRNA expression levels of Mcu, Micu1-3, Efhd1, which has recently been demonstrated to interact with Mcu and suppress uniporter activity (Meng et al , 2023; Delgado de la Herran et al , 2024), and Letm1, which is also suggested to play a role in Ca² ⁺ transport, along with its paralogue Letm2. Moreover, the mRNA levels of slc8b1 (the probable NCLX ortholog) and Tmem65, which has been recently considered to be involved in Ca 2+ efflux from mitochondria (Zhang et al , 2023; Vetralla et al , 2023; Garbincius et al , 2023) were tested. S lc25a28 , which encodes Mitoferrin-2, a Ca 2+ -independent transporter present in the mitochondrial inner membrane, served as control. This analysis showed that none of the examined members of the mitochondrial Ca 2+ transport system are differentially expressed in tmbim5 -/-zebrafish (Fig. EV3), suggesting that none of them compensate for the loss of Tmbim5.

No severe phenotype of fish lacking the NCLX ortholog Slc8b1 and Tmbim5

Sodium-dependent mitochondrial Ca 2+ release is mediated by NCLX (Palty et al , 2010). To investigate potential functional interactions between Nclx and Tmbim5, we next generated a double knockout zebrafish line lacking both Tmbim5 and the predicted Nclx. Similar to Mcu, we assumed that depletion of two (potentially independent) mitochondrial Ca 2+ release systems would be detrimental as shown in cell culture where loss of TMBIM5 with concurrent inactivation of NCLX by NCLX silencing or treatment with CGP resulted in a reduced mitochondrial Ca 2+ buffering capacity (Austin et al , 2022; Patron et al , 2022). The identity of NCLX in fish is unknown but the slcb81 gene encodes a 308 amino acid protein with the highest homology (24%) to human NCLX and a predicted sodium/calcium exchanger domain at its N-terminus (Fig. EV4A). Abolished behavioral response to CGP in Tmbim5-deficient zebrafish

To further investigate the potential interplay between Tmbim5 and Slc8b1 without risking damage to vulnerable mitochondria during isolation, we used behavioral changes induced by the Nclx inhibitor CGP as a readout in intact zebrafish. We found that CGP added to the bath reduced locomotor activity of wild-type zebrafish measured by velocity and total distance covered within a few hours. This allowed us to compare locomotor activity of wild-type, tmbim5and slc8b1 -deficient, and double-knockout larvae. We found no significant differences between genotypes in not-treated larvae (Fig. 6A). Surprisingly, WT, slc8b1 -/- and double tmbim5 -/-; slc8b1 -/-larvae exhibited a reduction in locomotor activity during a five-hour treatment while tmbim5 -/-mutant fish were unresponsive (Fig. 6A). These changes are specific to CGP, because WT and tmbim5 -/-mutant fish both reacted similarly to CCCP with an initial increase in locomotor activity, followed by a gradual reduction in mobility over the course of one hour (Fig 6B). These results suggest that Tmbim5 deficiency confers a partial protection against the effects of CGP in vivo . These results are unexpected, as they again contradict the hypothesis that Tmbim5 functions as an additional non-sodium-dependent mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux pathway. If this were the case, one would anticipate a more pronounced effect of CGP, which inhibits the remaining Nclx-mediated efflux, in the absence of Tmbim5. It is, however, in line with the reduced mortality of Slc8b1-depleted zebrafish in double tmbim5 -/-; slc8b1 -/KO fish. Together these results suggest that Slc8b1 is not the target of CGP and that neither Tmbim5 nor Slc8b1 constitutes the major mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux transport system in zebrafish.

Discussion

We found that Tmbim5, a less-studied inner mitochondrial membrane ion transporter, is essential for growth, muscle function, metabolism, and mitochondrial membrane potential in zebrafish, but not for steady-state mitochondrial Ca 2+ levels. Double knockout of Tmbim5 and major mitochondrial Ca 2+ transport systems caused no severe phenotype, suggesting that Tmbim5 does not compensate for their loss and other Ca 2+ transport mechanisms exist.

Tmbim5 deficiency decreased the size of zebrafish larvae and weight of adults in line with knockdown of the tmbim5 ortholog in snails (Yu et al , 2020) but not in mice (Zhang et al , 2022). However, similar to Tmbim5 mutant mice that suffer from myopathy, myofiber cross-sections were smaller in tmbim5 -/-fish, indicating muscle atrophy. Glycogen was reduced in skeletal muscle and liver of tmbim5 -/-fish and the intermuscular spaces were intensely stained with Alcian blue suggesting the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans. Both abnormalities probably correspond to ATP deficiency which has been reported as a common effect of TMBIM5 loss in cell lines (Seitaj et al , 2020; Patron et al , 2022; Zhang et al , 2022). Accumulation of glycosaminoglycans are also in line with mitochondrial dysfunction, impaired oxidative phosphorylation, and oxidative stress (Stepien et al , 2020)(Montero et al , 2019; Yubero et al , 2016)(Oller et al , 2021). These pathological alterations of muscle function probably underlie the delayed escape of Tmbim5-deficient embryos from the chorion which is mediated by tail coiling (Lessman & Carver, 2014). Interestingly, also Mcu knockout in mice negatively affects size (Pan et al , 2013) and affects proper skeletal muscle trophism (Mammucari et al , 2015; Gherardi et al , 2019). MICU1-deficient mice also showed severe growth defects (Liu et al , 2016) and marked muscle weakness, easy fatigue and smaller muscle fibers (Liu et al , 2016; Debattisti et al , 2019). In addition, loss-of-function mutations in human MICU1 are associated with myopathy (Logan et al , 2014; Lewis-Smith et al , 2016; Bitarafan et al , 2021). Together these observations corroborate the role of Tmbim5 in the regulation of growth and muscle function and point out similarities between the effects of Tmbim5, Mcu and Micu1 loss.

Two recent studies demonstrated that TMBIM5 functions as a pH-dependent mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux transporter (Patron et al , 2022; Austin et al , 2022). Zhang et al., in contrast, found that TMBIM5 overexpression in HEK cells enhanced mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake following ER Ca 2+ release (Zhang et al , 2022), suggesting that TMBIM5 may operate bidirectionally under certain conditions. To clarify the physiological relevance of this, we now generated two double knockout zebrafish lines: one lacking both Tmbim5 and Mcu, the primary mitochondrial Ca 2+ influx mediator, and another deficient in Tmbim5 and Slc8b1, the zebrafish ortholog of NCLX, major mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux pathway. Surprisingly, both double knockout lines were viable and exhibited no overt phenotypic alterations. Loss of Tmbim5 even protected against an increase in mortality observed in slc8b1 KO fish making it rather improbable that both proteins mediate mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux as lack of both pathways would probably trigger a pathological response. One possible mechanism of protection is the limitation of mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake. This protective effect may not necessarily be a direct consequence of Tmbim5 functioning as a Ca 2+ transporter, but Tmbim5 could modulate Ca 2+ influx through its interaction with the MCU complex. Recent evidence suggests that TMBIM5 is a member of the MCU interactome (Delgado de la Herran et al , 2024). Furthermore, TMBIM5 has been shown to inhibit the m-AAA protease subunit AFG3L2 (Patron et al , 2022), which is responsible for degrading EMRE (König et al , 2016; Tsai et al , 2017), a crucial component of the MCU complex required for its proper assembly and function. Therefore, the absence of Tmbim5 could lead to an overactivation of the m-AAA protease, resulting in increased EMRE degradation and, consequently, reduced Ca 2+ influx through the MCU complex.

Our contribution is the first to describe the presence, expression pattern and the loss-of-function phenotype of the probable NCLX ortholog encoded by slc8b1 in zebrafish. Similar to mouse NCLX (Luongo et al , 2017), zebrafish slc8b1 is mainly expressed in the nervous system. However, its knockout does not cause early embryonic lethality as has been described for the mouse ortholog (Luongo et al , 2017). However, also MCU deficiency causes no major phenotype in zebrafish (Soman et al , 2019b; Tomar et al , 2019; Langenbacher et al , 2020) but causes lethality in mice, at least in the C57Bl/6 background [Citation error]. It is noteworthy, that the protein encoded by zebrafish slc8b1 is considerably smaller than human NCLX, roughly half the size, making it possible that homodimers of zebrafish Slc8b1 could work as NCLX. At this time, we cannot rule out that zebrafish do not have a bona fide homolog. Also the similar effects of CGP, which reduced locomotor activity, in both wild-type zebrafish and in zebrafish lacking Slc8b1, suggest that Slc8b1 might not be the primary target of CGP in zebrafish. Alternatively, this could be caused by known pleiotropic effects of this compound, which also inhibits voltage-dependent Ca² ⁺ channels (Ruiz et al , 2014) and sarco/endoplasmic reticulum Ca² ⁺ -ATPase (SERCA) activity (Neumann et al , 2011), while activating ryanodine receptor channels (Neumann et al , 2011). Astonishingly, the double knockout of tmbim5 / slc8b1 reacted to CGP similar to wildtype and was not resistant to CGP like tmbim5 KO alone. Further investigation is needed to address the transport capacity of Slc8b1 in more detail and to elucidate the specific role of Tmbim5 in mediating the effects of CGP in zebrafish.

We conclude that this lack of a strong phenotype in double knockout fish for influx and efflux pathways suggests the presence of robust compensatory mechanisms or the existence of alternative pathways that can maintain mitochondrial Ca 2+ homeostasis in the absence of Tmbim5 and Mcu/Slc8b1. So which other proteins could do the job? LETM1 has been postulated to mediate mitochondrial H + -dependent Ca 2+ efflux (Jiang et al , 2009) and LETM1-containing liposomes were able to accumulate Ca 2+ (Jiang et al , 2009; Tsai et al , 2014; Shao et al , 2016), but its function as a Ca 2+ transporter is still in dispute. Austin et al. observed that inhibiting the potassium-hydrogen exchanger with quinine reduced mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux. They interpreted this finding as evidence that LETM1 might influence mitochondrial Ca 2+ efflux by directly regulating monovalent cation levels rather than by directly transporting Ca 2+ itself (Austin et al , 2017). Interestingly, LETM1 might interact with TMBIM5 (Austin et al , 2022). Another recently identified candidate for Ca 2+ efflux is TMEM65 (Garbincius et al , 2023; Vetralla et al , 2023; Zhang et al , 2023). However, TMEM65-mediated Ca 2+ efflux can also be inhibited by CGP as well as by NCLX knockout, indicating that this protein is an essential NCLX regulator and not an independent transporter (Garbincius et al , 2023; Vetralla et al , 2023).

In conclusion, loss of Tmbim5 impairs zebrafish growth, increases cell death in the brain and results in abnormal mitochondrial function as evidenced by reduced mitochondrial membrane potential and altered metabolism. However, the absence of Tmbim5 did not affect mitochondrial Ca 2+ levels or exacerbate the phenotype of either mcu or slc8b1 knockout zebrafish. This study demonstrated that mitochondrial Ca 2+ homeostasis can still be maintained in the absence of Tmbim5 and Mcu or Slc8b1. Thus, alternative pathway(s) of mitochondrial Ca 2+ influx and efflux compensate for the loss of these proteins.

Methods and Protocols:

Animal Maintenance

tmbim5 +/+ , tmbim5 -/-, tmbim5 +/+ ;Tg( HuC:CEPIA2mt ), tmbim5 -/-;Tg( HuC:CEPIA2mt ); tmbim5 +/+ ; mcu -/-, tmbim5 -/-; mcu -/-, tmbim5 +/+ ; slc8b1 -/-and tmbim5 -/-; slc8b1 -/-zebrafish were used in the study. All of the animals were maintained according to previously described methods (Matthews et al , 2002) in the Zebrafish Core Facility that is a licensed breeding and research facility (PL14656251, registry of the District Veterinary Inspectorate in Warsaw; 064 and 051, registry of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education) at the International Institute of Molecular and Cell Biology in Warsaw. All of the experiments with larvae and adult fish were performed in accordance with the Polish Laboratory Animal Science Association and Local Ethics Committee for Animal Experimentation in Warsaw (permission no. WAW2/050/2022). Adult zebrafish and larvae were kept in E3 medium (2.48 mM NaCl, 0.09 mM KCl, 0.164 mM CaCl2·2H2O, and 0.428 mM MgCl2·6H2O) at 28.5 °C. Larvae were kept in a Petri dish (~50 larvae/dish) in an incubator under a 14 h/10 h light/dark cycle. The stages of fish development were defined as hours postfertilization (hpf) and days postfertilization (dpf). Zebrafish embryos, that were planned to be used in stainings or in vivo imaging experiments, were treated with N-phenylthiourea (PTU, Alfa Aesar CAT# 103-85-5) at final concentration of 0.003% to avoid pigmentation. This study is reported in accordance with ARRIVE guidelines (Kilkenny et al , 2010).

Generation and genotyping of mutant and transgenic zebrafish lines

Generation of tmbim5 -/-zebrafish with CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing was previously described in (Gasanov et al , 2021). Briefly, a mix of two gRNA: 5'-GGCTGGTATGTGGGAGTCTA-3' and 5'-CGCGGGCAGTGTGGGCCTGA-3' (0.04 mg/ml of each) and Cas9 protein (0.2 mg/ml) were injected into the cytoplasm of one-cell stage wild-type AB zebrafish embryos.

Fish were grown up after injections up to 4-months-old and were fin-clipped for DNA sampling and high resolution melting (HRM) analysis. Targeted region in the 4 th exon of tmbim5 was amplified using Precision Melt Supermix (BioRad, Cat# 1725112), primers: tmbim5 HRM forward : 5'-CGATCTGGCCGCAGTACG-3', tmbim5 HRM reverse : 5'-CCAGCCAGGAGTTGCTC-3' and the CFX Connect RT-PCR Detection System (BioRad). Melting curve analysis was performed using Precision Melt Analysis (Bio-Rad) software. The mutation-positive ones were analyzed by DNA sequencing. Fish with the deletion of 29 nucleotides and premature STOP codon was selected as F0 founder. F0 fish was outcrossed with the AB zebrafish line, and their mutation-carrying offspring were in-crossed to generate homozygous mutants. Fish were screened via PCR using the same primers as for HRM analysis, followed by Sanger sequencing.

Genotyping of subsequent generations of tmbim5 +/+ , tmbim5 +/-and tmbim5 -/-fish was based on the difference in the size of PCR product that was detected by electrophoresis in 2% agarose.

To generate double knockout of tmbim5 and mcu , tmbim5-/- zebrafish were outcrossed with mcu -/-zebrafish (obtained as a kind gift from prof. Jacek Kuźnicki laboratory). Obtained heterozygous fish were incrossed and their offspring was genotyped for tmbim5 as described above and for mcu as described in (Soman et al , 2019a).

To generate zebrafish knockout of nclx the exon 5 of slc8b1 gene was targeted with gRNA designed with free web tools: CHOPCHOP (Labun et al , 2019) and CRISPRscan (Moreno-Mateos et al , 2015) according to the protocol described in (Wiweger et al , 2021). Sequences that were common in both of the predictions and were scored as low risk for off-targets were chosen. The correctness of the sequence in the targeted area in the AB

zebrafish was confirmed by Sanger sequencing. The gene-specific oligonucleotide with T7 overlaps: 5'-taatacgactcactataGGAGTGACGTTCCTGGCTCTgttttagagctagaa-3'. During gRNA preparation, annealing and filling in steps were combined, and the template was prepared by PCR using BioMix Red (BioLine, Cat# BIO-25006), gene-specific and constant oligo: 5'-AAAAGCACCGACTCGGTGCCACTTTTTCAAGTTGATAACGGACTAGCCTTATTTT AACTTGCTATTTCTAGCTCTAAAAC-3' (10 µM). The PCR conditions were the following: pre-incubation at 95°C for 3 min, followed by 35 cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 40°C for 15 s, and 68°C for 15 s. sgRNA was prepared using 150 ng of purified PCR product and MegaScript T7 Transcription Kit (Invitrogen, Cat# A57622) according to the manufacturer's recommendations. Thereafter, RNA was precipitated by overnight incubation with ammonium acetate at -20 o C.

gRNA was suspended in water, and the concentration was adjusted to 500 ng/μl. Cas9 protein (14 mg/ml stock, made in-house) was diluted in KCl/HEPES (200 mM/10 mM, pH 7.5) buffer to a final concentration of 600 ng/μl. The injection mixture was assembled fresh by mixing 2 μl of gRNA (1 μg), 2 μl of Cas9 (1.2 μg), and 0.5 μl phenol red (Sigma, Cat# P0290) and left for complex formation at room temperature for 5-10 min. Thereafter, the samples were kept on ice. Microneedles were pulled from borosilicate glass capillaries (Sutter; Cat# B100-75-10) using a P-1000 Flaming/Brown micropipette puller (Sutter). One nanoliter of the gRNA/Cas9/phenol red mixture was injected into the yolk at 1-2 cell-stage tmbim5 -/-zebrafish embryos using a FemtoJet microinjector (Eppendorf).

Fish were grown up after injections up to 4-months-old and were fin-clipped for DNA sampling and high resolution melting (HRM) analysis. Targeted region in the 5 th exon of scl8b1 was amplified using Precision Melt Supermix (BioRad, Cat# 1725112), primers: slc8b1 HRM forward : 5'-GTGTACCATGTGGATGTGTGTTG-3', slc8b1 HRM reverse : 5'-CAGACCAGCAGTTTGAGGGTG-3' and the CFX Connect RT-PCR Detection System (BioRad). Melting curve analysis was performed using Precision Melt Analysis (Bio-Rad) software. The mutation-positive ones were analyzed by DNA sequencing of PCR products. Samples for sequencing were prepared by PCR using BioMix Red (BioLine, Cat# BIO-25006), slc8b1 seq forward : 5'-GAGTTTGCCGTTTAATTTCTGG-3', slc8b1 seq reverse : 5'-AATTCCTCACCAAAAAGTGCTC-3' and 2 μl of gDNA template per 20 μl of reaction. Fish with the confirmed insertions or deletions leading to premature STOP codon were selected as F0 founders. F0 fish was outcrossed with the AB zebrafish line, and their mutation-carrying offspring were in-crossed to generate double-homozygous mutants. Fish were screened via HRM analysis, followed by Sanger sequencing.

For in vivo mitochondrial Ca 2+ imaging experiments tmbim5 -/-fish were outcrossed with Tg( HuC:CEPIA2mt ) zebrafish (obtained as a kind gift from prof. Jacek Kuźnicki laboratory). Their offspring were sorted for CEPIA2mt signal in the brain under the fluoroscope between 2 to 4 dpf.

Quantitative Real -Time PCR

Total RNA was extracted using TRI Reagent (Invitrogen, Cat# AM9738) according to a previously published protocol (Peterson & Freeman, 2009). Thirty embryos or larvae or organs dissected from two adult zebrafish were pooled together to obtain one RNA sample. RNA quality was verified by measuring absorbance at 260, 280, and 230 nm. Only samples with absorbance >1.8 at A260/280 nm were used for further processing. 500 ng of RNA was used for cDNA synthesis with iScript™ cDNA Synthesis Kit (BioRad, Cat# 1708891).

qRT-PCR was performed in duplicate using 25 ng of cDNA, SsoAdvanced Universal SYBR Green Supermix (BioRad, Cat# 1725274) and 0.25 μM of each primer. The primers that were used for qRT-PCR are listed in Table 1. Gene expression levels were analyzed using the CFX Connect Real-time PCR Detection System (Bio-Rad). Fold changes were calculated using the ΔΔCq method. Eukaryotic translation elongation factor 1 α1, like 1 ( ef1a ) and ribosomal protein L13a ( rpl13a ) or 18S ribosomal RNA ( 18S ) (for tmbim5 and slc8b1 expression analysis during the development) were used as housekeeping genes for normalization. Changes in expression are expressed as fold changes using expression in WT as a reference value. The calculations were performed in Microsoft Excel.

Whole-mount in situ hybridization (WISH)

Riboprobes for the genes of interest were obtained by PCR using specific primers with T7 overlap at 5' end of reverse: tmbim5 ISH forward : 5'-CGCTGCTCAGAGGTGAAGGT-3', tmbim5 ISH reverse : 5'-taatacgactcactatagggATGTGTGCGGCATCTCTGCG-3', slc8b1 ISH

forward : 5'-GTGTAGACGTGCGTTCTTGC-3', slc8b1 ISH reverse : 5'-taatacgactcactatagGG ACGAGACGCCACAGTAAA-3'. Purified PCR products were next utilized in in vitro transcription using MegaScript T7 Transcription Kit (Invitrogen, Cat# A57622) and digoxigenin (DIG)-labeled UTP (Roche, Cat# 11277073910) according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Zebrafish larvae were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA; Sigma-Aldrich, P6148) in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) overnight at room temperature and stored at -20 o C in the methanol. WISH was performed as previously described (Soman et al , 2019a) with several modifications. Briefly, after gradual rehydration with PBST (0.1% Tween-20 in PBS), permeabilization with 0.1% sodium citrate and 0.1% Triton X-100 for 10 min on ice, pre-hybridization in Hybridization Solution (HS; 50% deionized formamide, 5X sodium citrate buffer (SSC), 500 μg/ml tRNA, 50 μg/ml heparin, 0.1% Tween-20, 10mM citric acid, 5% sodium dextran sulfate) at 65 o C for 3 h, the probes against target mRNAs were added in HS (0.5-3 μg probe/ml buffer) and incubated overnight at 67 o C in the water bath. Next, larvae were washed with Hybridization Wash Solution (HWS; 50% deionized formamide, 5X SSC, 0.1% Tween-20, 10mM citric acid) and subsequently with 75% HWS, 50% HWS, 25% HWS in 2X SSCT (SSC with 0.1% Tween-20) and 0.2X SSCT at 65 o C. After blocking with 10% Blocking Reagent (Roche, Cat# 11096176001) in Maleic Acid buffer (100mM maleic acid, 100 mM NaCl, 50 mM MgCl2, 0.1% Tween-20) an alkaline phosphatase-conjugated anti-DIG antibody (Roche, Cat# 11093274910) was added to the blocking solution in 1:5000 dilution and incubated overnight at 4 o C. Signal was developed by incubation with nitro-blue tetrazolium chloride (0.45 mg/ml, Roche, Cat# 11383213001) and 5-bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl-phosphate (0.175 mg/ml, Roche, Cat# 11383221001) in Detection buffer (50 mM Tris-HCl pH 9.5, 50 mM NaCl, 25 mM MgCl2, 0.05% Tween 20, 2% polyvinyl alcohol) for 3 h. Background signal was cleaned by 10 min wash with 100% methanol and samples were re-fixed with 4% PFA for 20 min at room temperature and stored in the glycerol until imaging. Imaging was performed using the Nikon SMZ25 stereomicroscope.

Histology

Adult eight-month-old zebrafish were fixed in Bouin's solution, dehydrated in ethanol, xylene and embedded in paraffin. 6 μm thick longitudinal and transverse sections were cut with a Leica microtome (RM2265, Leica) and stained with standard hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). For the determination of glycogen content and mucopolysaccharides accumulation, combined Alcian blue and periodic acid-Schiff (AB/PAS) reactions were performed. The structure of the muscle and connective tissue was visualized using Masson's trichrome stain. Microscopic analysis was carried out using a Nikon Eclipse 90i microscope with a Nikon DS5-U1 camera (Nikon Corporation, Japan) and the computer image analysis system NIS-Elements AR (Nikon Corporation, Japan).

Tetramethylrhodamine ethyl ester (TMRE) microinjections

The stock solution of TMRE (Invitrogen™, Cat# T669) was prepared by dissolving in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# D8418) to a final concentration of 10 mM. Injection solution was prepared at the day of the experiment by diluting 1:5 stock solution in the water (final concentration: 2 mM) and 1 nl was injected into the yolk of embryos at 1-2 cell stage. Microinjections were performed the same as during generation of nclx -/-mutants. Embryos were then grown according to standard methods described in the 'Animal maintenance' section, but kept in the dark.

In vivo imaging with lightsheet fluorescence microscopy (LSFM)

Stained or transgenic larvae were anesthetized with 0.2 mg/ml Tricaine (Sigma, Cat# A-5040), mounted in 2% low-melting-point agarose (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# A9414) in a glass capillary and imaged under Zeiss Lightsheet Z.1 microscope using 40X objective (Zeiss) with excitation at 488 nm (AO and Tg(CEPIA2mt) ) or 561 nm (TMRE). Fish were imaged from the dorsal side of the head and were pushed from capillary to be submerged in the medium from the top of the head up to the middle of the swimming bladder. Measurements were performed at room temperature.

Images were firstly processed in ZEN software (Zeiss) in order to obtain merged maximum projection images, exported to TIF files and analyzed further with ImageJ.

Cell death estimation by Acridine Orange in vivo staining: Acridine Orange (AO; Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# A8097) was added into the plates containing live 4 dpf zebrafish larvae (final concentration: 10 µg/ml E3). After 1 hour of incubation in the dark, the medium was exchanged to fresh E3 six times during 30 min. Z-stacks encompassing 45 µm of optic tectum with 5 µm interval were acquired with Zeiss Lightsheet Z.1 microscope. The counting of the AO-stained cells in the optic tectum of each larvae, that were considered as dying (Pyati et al , 2011), was performed manually on the maximum projection images with the CellCounter plug-in in the ImageJ. The number of cells was normalized to the analyzed area of the optic tectum.

Mitochondrial Ca 2+ flux in the brain of alive zebrafish: In order to estimate mitochondrial Ca 2+ content 5 dpf larvae, that expressed CEPIA2mt, Ca 2+ probe targeted to the mitochondria under neural promoter HuC, were treated with 10 μM CCCP (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# C2759) to induce depolarization of mitochondria and release of mitochondrial Ca 2+ . Treatment with 5 μM CGP-37157 (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# 220005) started 5 h before larvae were immobilized. After mounting the zebrafish larvae in the microscopic chamber filled with E3 the medium in the chamber was exchanged to E3 with 10 μM CCCP (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# C2759). To gain complete depolarization, larvae were treated for 10 min. Z-stacks encompassing 10 µm of optic tectum with 1 µm interval were acquired with Zeiss Lightsheet Z.1 microscope every 5 s during 10 min of treatment.

During image analysis The StackReg plug-in was applied for movement correction. Mitochondria were detected by thresholding and mean fluorescence for mitochondria in the selected square ROI in the optic tectum was calculated. Background fluorescence was calculated as the mean fluorescence of a square that was localized besides the fish, and subtracted from the mean fluorescence of ROI value: F = FROI - Fbackground. F was normalized by the value obtained after full depolarization (F/F CCCP ) and the ratio between basal fluorescence (F0 ) and FCCCP was calculated to compare mitochondrial Ca 2+ content between variants similarly to the method described in (Stavsky et al , 2021).

Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) in vivo measurements: Two days after microinjections with TMRE dechorionated larvae were imaged using LSFM as described above. 2 dpf larvae were treated with 10 μM CCCP (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# C2759) to induce depolarization of mitochondria. To gain complete depolarization, larvae were treated for 5 min, due to higher toxicity of CCCP treatment at this stage than at 5 dpf. Z-stacks encompassing 10 µm of optic tectum with 1 µm interval were acquired with Zeiss Lightsheet Z.1 microscope every 5 s during 5 min of treatment. F was normalized by the value obtained after full depolarization (F/F CCCP ) and the ratio between basal fluorescence (F0) and FCCCP was calculated to compare mitochondrial membrane potential between variants. Image analysis was performed as for mitochondrial Ca 2+ measurements described above.

Isolation zebrafish larvae mitochondria

To obtain functional mitochondria from zebrafish 5 dpf larvae a modified method described in (Prudent et al , 2013) was used. Larvae (300 - 450 per genotype) were sacrificed by Tricaine treatment and washed with the ice-cold Ringer's buffer (1.8 mM CaCl2, 116 mM NaCl, 2.9 mM KCl, 5 mM HEPES, pH 7.5). Next, rinsed larvae were incubated on ice in the mitochondria isolation buffer (MIB, 210 mM mannitol, 70 mM sucrose, 1mM EDTA, 10mM HEPES, pH 7.5) supplemented with 2 mM PMSF just before usage and transferred to precooled glass-teflon homogenizer in MIB with 2 mM PMSF and BSA (2 mg/ml). Zebrafish tissue was manually homogenized on ice with 50x strokes and centrifuged (1,500 x g, 10 min). Supernatant was transferred to a new tube and centrifuged again to eliminate nuclei and cell debris. After the high-speed centrifugation (14,000 x g, 15 min) of the obtained supernatant, the pellets containing the mitochondria were washed twice with 1 ml ice-cold KCl medium (125 mM KCl, 2 mM K2HPO4, 1 mM MgCl2, 5 mM glutamate, 5 mM malic acid, 20 mM HEPES, pH 7). Pellet was gently resuspended with 0.1 μl of KCl medium per larvae and protein amount was established by Bradford protein assay. During the whole procedure samples were kept on the ice, all centrifugations steps were performed at 4 o C and all used tubes and media were precooled on the ice.

Ca 2+ measurements in mitochondria isolated from zebrafish larvae

Mitochondrial Ca 2+ fluxes was measured in freshly isolated mitochondria from 5 dpf zebrafish larvae. 25 µg of mitochondria was gently suspended in KCl medium with glutamate (5 mM), malic acid (5 mM) added freshly and transferred into 96-well half-area black polystyrene microplate (Corning®, Cat# CLS3694). Thapsigargin (0.2 µM, Invitrogen™, Cat# T7459) was added in order to inhibit SERCA pump from potential contaminants from endoplasmic reticulum in the samples. The bath [Ca 2+ ] was detected using the Ca 2+ -sensitive dye Oregon Green™ 488 BAPTA-5N hexapotassium salt (2 µM, Invitrogen™, Cat# O6812) as described in (Soman et al , 2019a). The signal was measured using a plate reader (Tecan Infinite M1000 Pro) at Ex/Em 494/521 nm. Measurements were performed at room temperature.

For the analysis, the first read-out of baseline recording was defined as F0 and used for normalization. All traces are shown as ΔF/F 0 , where ΔF = F - F 0 .

Mitochondrial Ca 2+ uptake assay: 20 µM CaCl2 diluted in KCl medium was added with a multichannel pipette after 30 s of baseline recording. Signal was measured every 3 s for 10 min. Ca 2+ uptake was calculated as the difference between maximal ΔF/F0 (first read-out after CaCl 2 addition) and ΔF/F0 value measured 2 min after CaCl2 addition. For the statistical analysis, obtained Ca 2+ uptake measures were then normalized by the values obtained for WT from the same experiment (samples obtained during one mitochondria isolation process).

Behavioral analysis

Coiling activity of zebrafish embryos: Randomly selected embryos were transferred to a 12-well plate (4 embryos/well) in 2 ml of E3 and acclimated for 2h in 28 o C. Coiling activity was recorded 30 hpf using the Nikon SMZ25 stereomicroscope for 3 min with 10 frames /s acquisition rate. The number of coiling events for each embryo was counted manually.

Open field test: Randomly selected 4 dpf larvae were acclimated to the behavioral testing room for at least 15 min. Two minutes before recording locomotor activity, the larvae were transferred to a 12-well plate that was then placed in the ZebraBox, a high-throughput monitoring system (ViewPoint). The experiment was performed in a volume of 2 mL of E3 medium, and the light intensity was set to 70%. Locomotor activity was recorded for 10 min.

Visual motor response: The experiment was performed according to the procedure described previously (Kedra et al , 2020). On the day before the experiment, the larvae were placed in 24-well plates that contained 0.5 mL of E3 medium. Thirty minutes before recording locomotor activity, the plates were placed in the ZebraBox. The experiment consisted of three phases of the following changes in lighting conditions: baseline (0-10 min, 0% light intensity), light (10-20 min, 70% light intensity), and dark (20-30 min, 0% light intensity).

Locomotor response to CGP-37157 treatment: The stock solution of CGP-37157 (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# 220005) was prepared by dissolving in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# D8418) to a final concentration of 15 mM. On the day of the experiment, the larvae were placed in 24-well plates that contained E3 medium. All wells were filled up to 0.5 ml with a working solution of CGP-37157 or DMSO (both diluted 1:30 in E3, final concentration = 5 μM) and kept in 28 o C. 1, 2, 4 and 5 hours after treatment start plates were placed in the ZebraBox for 15 min and locomotor activity of larvae was recorded for 10 min after acclimatization period.

Locomotor response to CCCP treatment: The stock solution of CCCP (Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# C2759) was prepared by dissolving in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich, Cat# D8418) to a final concentration of 50 mM. On the day of the experiment, the larvae were placed in 24-well plates that contained CCCP diluted in E3 to the final concentration of 0.5 μM, 1 μM and 5 μM. Immediately after beginning of the treatment plates were placed in the ZebraBox for and locomotor activity of larvae was recorded for 60 min.

Novel tank test for adult zebrafish: The 8-months-old zebrafish were acclimated to the behavioral testing room for one week before the experiment. Fish were placed individually in a transparent tank (24 cm length x 14 cm height x 6cm width) filled with aquarium water. ZebraCube, an adult fish monitoring system (ViewPoint) was utilized to record animals' behavior for 15 min.

The video files acquired during experiments with larvae and adult zebrafish were further analyzed using EthoVision XT software (Noldus).

Statistical analysis

All statistical analyses were conducted using R version 4.1.2. Box and whisker plots were generated with R, while other graphs were generated with Microsoft Excel. All datasets were tested for outliers using a Grubbs's test and for normal distribution using the Shapiro-Wilk test. The applied statistical tests are indicated in the figure legends.

metadata.preprint_title: Tmbim5 loss causes muscle atrophy in zebrafish without exacerbating mcu or slc8b1 knockout phenotypes

pub_metadata.preprint_title: Tmbim5 loss causes muscle atrophy in zebrafish without exacerbating mcu or slc8b1 knockout phenotypes
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Wasilewska, I.; Adamek-Urbanska, D.; Baranykova, S.; Majewski, L.; Methner, A.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Mitochondrial calcium homeostasis involves coordinated uptake via the mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU) and efflux through sodium-dependent NCLX (encoded by SLC8B1). Mild Mcu knockout phenotypes suggest additional transport mechanisms. We investigated TMBIM5, a proposed bidirectional mitochondrial calcium/proton transporter, by generating zebrafish lacking tmbim5, slc8b1, plus tmbim5/mcu and tmbim5/slc8b1 double knockouts. Tmbim5-deficient fish exhibited growth impairment, muscle atrophy, and increased brain cell death. tmbim5/mcu double knockouts showed no additive effects, arguing against Tmbim5 functioning as an independent calcium uptake pathway. However, tmbim5/slc8b1 double knockouts showed disrupted mitochondrial calcium handling with reduced uptake and efflux. Remarkably, brain phenotypes were rescued while muscle dysfunction was exacerbated in double mutants, corresponding to restored mitochondrial membrane potential in Tmbim5-deficient brain tissue and exacerbated decreased calcium levels in double knockout muscle. We also found broad downregulation of mitochondrial calcium transport proteins and decreased mitochondrial DNA content in double knockout brain tissue, indicating reduced mitochondrial mass and a potential beneficial glycolytic shift in energy metabolism. These findings demonstrate that TMBIM5 functions as a calcium efflux pathway cooperating with NCLX in a tissue-specific manner.
